name: Prophecy of the Popes
description: The Prophecy of the Popes is a series of 112 short, cryptic phrases in Latin, purportedly predicting the popes from Pope Celestine II (elected in 1143) until the end of time. Attributed to Saint Malachy, Archbishop of Armagh in the 12th century, the prophecies were first published in 1595 by Benedictine historian Arnold Wion. Each phrase is said to describe a characteristic, birthplace, or coat of arms of a particular pope. Proponents argue that many phrases accurately match details of past popes, offering this as proof of the prophecy's validity. They often interpret the final prophecies, particularly those concerning 'Petrus Romanus,' as forecasting the end of the papacy or the world. Mainstream scholars and the Catholic Church generally dismiss the Prophecy of the Popes as a late 16th-century forgery. They point to the lack of any mention of the prophecies before their publication, the vagueness of the phrases, and the ease with which they can be retroactively applied to past popes. The specific Latin phrases are often open to multiple interpretations, making them susceptible to confirmation bias. Furthermore, the historical context surrounding the initial publication suggests a political motive, potentially aiming to influence a papal election. While the prophecy holds significant interest for those fascinated by eschatology and conspiracy theories, its historical and theological legitimacy remains highly questionable.
subtitle: A series of cryptic papal predictions.

claims.claim: The prophecies accurately predict characteristics of past popes.
claims.evidence: Many phrases seem to correspond to the birthplace, coat of arms, or reign of past popes.
claims.criticism: The phrases are vague and open to interpretation, allowing for retroactive fitting. Confirmation bias plays a significant role.

claims.claim: The prophecy originates from Saint Malachy in the 12th century.
claims.evidence: Attribution to Saint Malachy and the aura of mystery surrounding the text.
claims.criticism: No mention of the prophecies exists before their publication in 1595, casting doubt on their authenticity and early origin.

claims.claim: The prophecy predicts the end of the papacy with 'Petrus Romanus'.
claims.evidence: The final prophecy mentioning 'Petrus Romanus' is interpreted by some as indicating the end of the papacy or the world.
claims.criticism: The interpretation of 'Petrus Romanus' is speculative and lacks definitive historical or theological basis. The prophecy itself could be interpreted in numerous ways.

keyFigures.name: H.B. Hyde
keyFigures.role: Author of books promoting the validity of the prophecies.

mythologyConnections: Oracles and seers in ancient mythology

timeline.event: Election of Pope Celestine II, the first pope mentioned in the prophecies.
Traditional (but unverified) date of Malachy's vision.
Publication of the Prophecy of the Popes by Arnold Wion in his book 'Lignum Vitae'.

sources.author: M.J. O'Brien
sources.work: An Historical and Critical Account of S. Malachy, Archbishop of Armagh
sources.title: M.J. O'Brien
